Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese [[:en:Yuzu|yuzu]]-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the [[Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kamiyo Ame Candy]][[en:draft:Kamiyo Ame Candy]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kamiyo Ame Candy|Kamiyo Ame Candy]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese [[:en:Yuzu|yuzu]]-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the [[Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref> }}

Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the [[Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the [[Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|参道で加美代飴を売る「傘の下商人」]] '''加美代飴'''（かみよあめ）は、[[香川県]][[琴平町]]の[[金刀比羅宮]]の参道で販売されている[[扇子|扇]]型の飴{{R|朱|る}}。
== 概要 == [[べっこう飴]]であり、付属の小さいハンマーで砕き割って食べる{{R|朱|る}}。
「加美代」は「[[神代 (日本神話)|神代]]」にちなんで名づけられた{{R|和}}。
大門を入ってすぐの参道で5軒の飴屋が加美代飴が販売しているが、境内での営業が許可されている5軒は「五人百姓」と呼ばれる{{R|和|コ}}。傘を立てて大門内での飴売りは「傘の下商人」と呼ばれ、[[文化 (元号)|文化年間]]に編纂された『金毘羅参詣名所図会』にも描かれている{{R|和}}。なお、加美代飴そのものは境内以外でも店頭売りされている。
== 歴史 == [[江戸時代]]、金刀比羅宮参詣は[[伊勢神宮]]参詣と同様に人気が高かった{{R|和}}。
金刀比羅宮の[[土産菓子]]としては境内で販売されていた[[飴]]の人気が高く、『玉藻集』（[[延宝]]5年（[[1677年]]）、[[小西可春]]著）には「金比羅の丸糖」の表記がある{{R|和}}。当時は金刀比羅宮に備えられた米のお下がりで飴が作られていたとも言われる{{R|和}}。
第二次世界大戦後は、砂糖から作られた飴にと変わり、丸型の「こんぴら飴」として販売されていたが、類似商品が出回ったことから、1950年頃に扇型の加美代飴に改められた{{R|和}}。
== 出典 == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|和書|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[地球の歩き方]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|和書|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTBパブリッシング]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|和書|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[二見書房]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|和書|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTBパブリッシング]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref>
}}

金刀比羅宮の[[土産菓子]]としては境内で販売されていた[[飴]]の人気が高く、『玉藻集』（[[延宝]]5年（[[1677年]]）、[[小西可春]]著）には「金比羅の丸糖」の表記がある{{R|和}}。当時は金刀比羅宮に備えられた米のお下がりで飴が作られていたとも言われる{{R|和}}。
第二次世界大戦後は、砂糖から作られた飴にと変わり、丸型の「こんぴら飴」として販売されていたが、類似商品が出回ったことから、1950年頃に扇型の加美代飴に改められた{{R|和}}。
== 出典 == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|和書|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[地球の歩き方]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|和書|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTBパブリッシング]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|和書|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[二見書房]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|和書|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTBパブリッシング]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref>
}}
== 外部リンク == *[https://www.kotohirakankou.jp/souvenir/shop/entry-154.html 池商店] - 琴平町観光協会

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}

{{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese [[:en:Yuzu|yuzu]]-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref> 

The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

==Japanese content==

'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[:en:fan|fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.

== Overview ==
This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.

The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}

Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.

== History ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.

Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.

After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

== References ==
{{Reflist|refs=
<ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref>
<ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref>
<ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref>
<ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref>
}}

==Japanese content==

'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[:en:fan|fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.

== Overview ==
This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.

The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}

Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.

== History ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.

Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.

After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref> 

The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

{{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese [[:en:Yuzu|yuzu]]-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref> 

The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

==Japanese content==

'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[:en:fan|fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.

== Overview ==
This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.

The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}

Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.

== History ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.

Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.

After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

{{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese [[:en:Yuzu|yuzu]]-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref> 

The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

==Japanese content==

'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[:en:fan|fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.

== Overview ==
This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.

The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}

Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.

== History ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.

Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.

After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

== References ==
{{Reflist|refs=
<ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref>
<ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref>
<ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref>
<ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref>
}}

{{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese [[:en:Yuzu|yuzu]]-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref> 

The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

==Japanese content==

'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[:en:fan|fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.

== Overview ==
This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.

The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}

Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.

== History ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.

Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.

After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

== Overview ==
This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.

The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}

Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.

== History ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.

Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.

After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref> 

The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira, Kagawa]] Prefecture. It is shaped like a [[:en:fan|fan]]{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref> }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]]
to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kamiyo Ame Candy]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kamiyo Ame Candy|Kamiyo Ame Candy]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese {{ill|Yuzu|en|Yuzu|WD=Q867776|lt=yuzu}}-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira, Kagawa}} Prefecture. It is shaped like a {{ill|fan|en|fan|WD=Q293805|lt=fan}}{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref> }}

{{Expand Japanese}}[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese {{ill|Yuzu|en|Yuzu|WD=Q867776|lt=yuzu}}-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira, Kagawa}} Prefecture. It is shaped like a {{ill|fan|en|fan|WD=Q293805|lt=fan}}{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref> }}
== External Links == * [https://www.kotohirakankou.jp/souvenir/shop/entry-154.html Ikeshōten (Ike Shop)] - Kotohira Town Tourism Association

'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira, Kagawa}} Prefecture. It is shaped like a {{ill|fan|en|fan|WD=Q293805|lt=fan}}{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}

[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese {{ill|Yuzu|en|Yuzu|WD=Q867776|lt=yuzu}}-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:加美代飴]]
[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese {{ill|Yuzu|en|Yuzu|WD=Q867776|lt=yuzu|1=Yuzu}}-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira, Kagawa|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}} Prefecture. It is shaped like a {{ill|fan|en|fan|WD=Q293805|lt=fan|1=Fan}}{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref> }}

A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />

[[File:Kotohira Kotohira-gu Goninbyakushou 1.JPG|thumb|right|"Kasano-shita shōnin" (merchants under umbrellas) selling Kamiyo-ame along the approach to Kotohira-gū]]'''Kamiyo Ame''' is a traditional Japanese {{ill|Yuzu|en|Yuzu|WD=Q867776|lt=yuzu|1=Yuzu}}-flavored hard candy associated with [[Kotohira-gū]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Kamiyo Ame Candy|url=https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=おもてなしセレクション（OMOTENASHI Selection）|language=en-US}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kagawa {{!}} Pilgrimage to the Kotohira-gu Shrine {{!}} Authentic Japan: Setouchi|url=https://www.setouchi.travel/en/see-and-do/kotohira-gu-shrine/|access-date=2023-09-17|website=www.setouchi.travel|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-04-05|title=Once-in-a-lifetime Konpira-san! Let's visit Kotohira Shrine|url=https://thegate12.com/article/205|access-date=2023-09-17|website=THE GATE}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Konpirasan – the famous Pilgrimage of Shikoku (Kagawa)|url=http://setouchifinder.com/en/detail/4242?pg=2|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Stouchi Finder|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kotohira-gu|url=https://ikidane-nippon.com/en/spots/kotohira-gu|access-date=2023-09-17|website=Ikidane Nippon|language=en-US}}</ref> commonly known as Konpira-san, a Shinto shrine in Kagawa Prefecture. This confection has been offered at the shrine for over 700 years, symbolizing good fortune and divine blessings.<ref name=":1">https://omotenashinippon.jp/selection/en/prize/2200-02-056/</ref>
The candy is notable for its golden, amber-like appearance, reflecting the shrine's aesthetic. Traditionally, Kamiyo Ame is sold by five families known as "Gonin Byakusho" (Five Farmers), who have exclusive rights to vend the candy within the shrine's precincts. These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
==Japanese content==
'''Kamiyo-ame''' (加美代飴) is a type of [[amber candy (bekkō-ame)|bekkō-ame]] (amber candy) sold along the approach to [[Kotohira-gū]] in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira, Kagawa|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}} Prefecture. It is shaped like a {{ill|fan|en|fan|WD=Q293805|lt=fan|1=Fan}}{{R|朱|る}}.
== Overview == This candy is a type of [[bekkō-ame]] (amber candy), and it is traditionally eaten by breaking it into pieces with a small hammer that comes with it{{R|朱|る}}.
The name “Kamiyo” (加美代) is derived from “kamiyo” (神代), meaning the “Age of the Gods.”{{R|和}}
Five candy shops sell Kamiyo-ame just past the large gate (ōmon) on the approach. These five shops, which are permitted to do business within the shrine grounds, are known as the “Gonin Hyakushō” (Five Merchants){{R|和|コ}}. The vendors who sell candy under umbrellas inside the main gate are called “kasa no shita shōnin” (umbrella-shade merchants) and are depicted in the “Konpira Sankei Meisho Zue,” an illustrated guide to Konpira pilgrimages compiled during the [[Bunka era (1804–1818)|Bunka period]]{{R|和}}. Although Kamiyo-ame is known for being sold within the shrine grounds, it is also sold at shops outside the shrine grounds.
== History == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, visiting [[Kotohira-gū]] (Konpira-san) was as popular as visiting the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]]{{R|和}}.
Among the souvenir confections of Kotohira-gū, candy sold in the shrine grounds was especially popular. In the “Tamao-shū” (compiled in [[Enpō 5 (1677)|Enpō 5 (1677)]] by Konishi Kaharu), the candy is referred to as “Konpira no Marudō” (丸糖, round candy){{R|和}}. It is said that at that time, the candy was made using rice offered at Kotohira-gū{{R|和}}.
After World War II, the candy, which had been made from rice, was changed to a sugar-based candy called “Konpira-ame” (round candy). However, as similar products began appearing, around 1950 the shape was changed to a fan shape and renamed “Kamiyo-ame.”{{R|和}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="朱">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=45|chapter=金刀比羅宮のパワスポめぐり|title=御朱印でめぐる四国の神社週末開運さんぽ|year=2021|publisher=[[Chikyū no Arukikata]]|isbn=978-4059195023}}</ref> <ref name="る">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=74|chapter=境内を巡る|title=るるぶ香川高松琴平小豆島直島'24|year=2023|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533153990}}</ref> <ref name="和">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=89|chapter=加美代飴|title=美しい和菓子の図鑑|author=青木直己|year=2021|publisher=[[Futami Shobō]]|isbn=978-4576210636}}</ref> <ref name="コ">{{Cite book|language=ja|page=28|chapter=四国を代表するパワースポットこんぴらさんへお参り|title=ココミル 四国|year=2014|publisher=[[JTB Publishing]]|isbn=978-4533098888}}</ref> }}
== External Links == * [https://www.kotohirakankou.jp/souvenir/shop/entry-154.html Ikeshōten (Ike Shop)] - Kotohira Town Tourism Association

These families have maintained the confection's production methods and cultural significance through generations. <ref name=":1" />
A unique aspect of Kamiyo Ame is its consumption method: the candy is broken into pieces using a small golden hammer, allowing it to be shared among individuals. This practice embodies the communal and auspicious nature of the confection. <ref>https://guidememo.com/konpira/</ref><ref name=":0" />
